evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns and manipulative design practices
the preservation of purchased digital games when publishers shut down online services.
where publishers design games to require constant online connectivity even when offline play would be technically possible.

paragraphs.text: Dear European Commission, I strongly support the Digital Fairness Act initiative and appreciate the Commission's efforts to protect consumers in our digital economy. The measures addressing dark patterns and manipulative design practices are exactly what we need to restore balance between consumers and digital platforms. I would like to highlight an important consumer protection issue that should be included in this Act: the preservation of purchased digital games when publishers shut down online services. Currently, when I buy a digital game for 60, the publisher can make it completely unusable by shutting down their servers, even though I legally own the product. This happened recently with Ubisoft's racing game "The Crew" - thousands of European consumers lost access to a game they had purchased, with no compensation or alternative access method provided. This practice is fundamentally unfair. Imagine buying a physical appliance and having the manufacturer remotely disable it years later simply because they no longer want to support it. Yet this happens routinely in the gaming industry, where publishers design games to require constant online connectivity even when offline play would be technically possible. The problem isn't just about individual games - it's about the principle of digital ownership. When European consumers purchase digital products, we expect them to function for a reasonable period, just like any other consumer good. The current situation undermines consumer confidence and allows publishers to effectively steal back products they've already sold. Many games could easily function offline or allow community-run servers, but publishers deliberately prevent this to maintain control. Some publishers have shown it's possible to provide alternatives when discontinuing services - they simply choose not to in most cases. The Digital Fairness Act should address this by requiring publishers to ensure purchased games remain accessible when they discontinue services. This could mean providing offline modes, allowing community servers, or simply not designing artificial online dependencies in the first place. Publishers should also be required to clearly inform consumers at purchase if a game depends on services that might be discontinued. This issue affects millions of European gamers and represents billions of euros in consumer investments. Including game preservation protections would align perfectly with existing EU consumer rights principles and demonstrate that digital ownership means something real, not just a temporary license that can be revoked at will. The technical solutions exist - what we need is the regulatory framework to ensure publishers use them. This would encourage more sustainable business practices while protecting consumers' legitimate ownership rights in the digital age. Thank you for considering this important addition to the Digital Fairness Act. Protecting digital game ownership would significantly strengthen the legislation's consumer protection impact. Sincerely, A concerned European consumer
